mappings.rationaleAI: The Holy Roman Empire's Investiture Controversy, Hohenstaufen expansion, and feudal fragmentation directly address the Holy Roman Empire, the papacy, and Italy from c. 1050 to c. 1300 (A.2) and Germany, Bohemia, and the Swiss Confederation (A.4.a).
Joan of Arc's lifting of the siege of Orleans and securing Charles VII's coronation directly address recovery and reunification and Joan of Arc's role in stirring French national feeling (A.6.a.v).
The Renaissance's humanism, classical recovery, and developments in art and literature directly address the early Renaissance in Italy (A.1.a).
The Middle Ages covering feudalism, Church, Crusades, scholasticism, and Black Death broadly addresses the entire scope of Section 923.
France's Capetian medieval monarchy directly addresses the growth of the Kingdom of France under the later Capetians (A.3) and France and the Low Countries in the later Middle Ages (A.6).
Italy's medieval city-states and papal power directly address Italy within the Holy Roman Empire context (A.2) and Italy in the late Middle Ages and Renaissance (A.5).
Spain's Reconquista and the union of Aragon and Castile directly address the Spanish Christian kingdoms (A.4) and Spain and Portugal in the later Middle Ages (A.8).
The Black Death killing 30-60% of Europe's population directly addresses the economic, social, and political effects of the Black Death (1347-50) and subsequent epidemics (A.2.b).
Poland's medieval kingdom and Piast dynasty directly address the Slavic and Magyar states (A.7.a) and Poland-Lithuania and the Teutonic Order (A.10.b).
The Viking Age's settlements and political consolidation provide background context for the Scandinavian kingdoms (A.6.a) and the formation of England and France.
The Crusades' commercial effects and financing connect to the revival of a money economy and the commercial effects of the Crusades (A.1.b).
The Byzantine Empire's interaction with Italian commercial cities and the Fourth Crusade (1204) connect to the growth of communes in northern Italy and Venetian expansion (A.2.a.iii).
The Mongol invasion of Hungary (1241) and Poland directly address the Mongol invasions and their impact on eastern European states (A.10.b.i).
Leonardo's work exemplifies new concepts and techniques in painting, sculpture, and architecture during the early Italian Renaissance (A.1.a.ii).
The Reformation, while primarily 16th century, has roots in late medieval reform movements including Wycliffe and Hus, connecting to the church in the later Middle Ages (A.3).

The Spanish Empire's origins under Ferdinand and Isabella, including Spanish explorations and colonial policy, directly address the creation of united Spain and expansion in the early Age of Discovery (A.8.c).
Islamic Golden Age intellectual achievements transmitted to Europe through translations connect to the intellectual revival and Scholasticism (A.1.e.ii).
The post-classical period broadly addresses the entire scope of Western Christendom in the High and Later Middle Ages (A).
